stimulus:
The following text is from a 1902 poem by the American writer Edith Hollowell, titled “The Lamp.”

As a small and steady lamp keeps its slender flame
 in the chamber’s quiet air, if
 the patient hand pour in fresh oil at evening;
So doth my listening soul give light, if it be tended with the
 steady oil of friendship,
Lacking this, it presently grows wan and falters,
 even as a lamp left dry upon the table, if the
 hand of the keeper attend it not.
stem: Based on the text, in what way is the listening soul like a lamp?

answerOptions.content: It grows steadier and brighter with the passage of years.
It depends on regular care if it is to keep giving light.
It draws its strength from sudden gusts of air.
It endures unchanged through every kind of neglect.

rationale:
Choice B is the best answer. The text says the lamp keeps its flame only if a patient hand pours in fresh oil, and the soul gives light only if it is tended with the “steady oil of friendship”; lacking this care, both grow wan and falter. Both therefore depend on regular care to keep giving light.

Choice A is incorrect because the text does not address the passage of years or claim that the soul grows brighter over time. Choice C is incorrect because the text never describes gusts of air as a source of strength. Choice D is incorrect because the text says the opposite — without tending, both lamp and soul falter at once.
